Name: Myra Deng
Question 1 Response: DPO Training for Mathematical Problem Solving
Question 6 Response:
Myra had the main idea for the project and was particularly interested in how DPO training could enhance mathematical problem-solving. She took the lead in setting up the computational experiments, preparing the Jupyter notebooks for training and evaluation, and ensuring that the integration of preference-based alignment techniques proceeded smoothly. Myra’s focus on identifying and incorporating prior work provided valuable context, shaping the project’s objectives and methodological considerations. Myra also chose the training data and interpreted  initial results were to guiding subsequent adjustments to the training pipeline.

Chris managed the technical infrastructure that underpinned the experiments. By setting up the GPU instance in Azure—a Standard NC4as T4 v3 configuration—Chris ensured that the training environment was stable, efficient, and compatible with the chosen frameworks. He also handled data compilation and took lead on preparing documentation and presentation of findings.

Chris and Myra worked together in the actual training of the model and running the evaluations on the three models against the benchmark.

Name: Olivia Beyer Bruvik
Question 1 Response: Optimizing Sea Lice Management in Norwegian Salmon Aquaculture Using Q-Learning
Question 6 Response: In this project, Will focused on simulating lice levels in a model-free approach. This simulation allows us to train and evaluate our policies. Olivia worked on implementing the Q-learning model with a binary action space $A = \{\text{No treatment}, \text{ Mechanical only}\}$. The initial Q-learning model with a binary action space allowed us to explore and establish an appropriate reward function. She also implemented and explored the performance of Q-learning with value function approximation. Learning that the Q-learning performed worse with value function approximation, we shifted our focus to our exact Q-learning. Ali expanded this Q-learning model to include the complete action space with eight possible treatment combinations, and worked on evaluating the Q-learning agent against a random policy. These experiments allowed us to better understand which action spaces, reward models, and hyperparameters increased the performance of the Q-learning agent. All three members contributed to various components of the final paper, and each spent an extra 30 hours making sure the paper is conference-ready.

Name: Xuwen Yao
Question 1 Response: Algorithmic Trading for IMC Prosperity 2
Question 6 Response:
The success of our project on developing an advanced algorithmic trading agent for the "Prosperity" simulation game is the result of collaborative efforts and specialized contributions from each team member. Their roles and responsibilities were as follows:

Jialiang Xu: As the idea provider for the project, Jialiang played a role in conceptualizing the use of Q-learning for the trading agent. Jialiang led the coding part of the project, which involved developing the core algorithmic framework, running experiments, and tuning parameters to optimize the agent's performance in the trading simulation. His technical expertise was instrumental in implementing the learning models and in the practical application of theoretical concepts to our project’s needs.

Xuwen Yao: Xuwen was responsible for driving the project workflow and ensuring that all aspects of the project were aligned with our goals. This included conducting extensive research to identify relevant academic papers and resources that could enhance our understanding and development of the trading agent. Xuwen took the lead in drafting the proposal, providing updates, and compiling the final paper, effectively communicating our findings and methodologies. Her organizational skills and attention to detail ensured that our project adhered to timelines and academic standards.

Name: Selina Zang
Question 1 Response: Dual-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Strategy Optimization in Gomoku
Question 6 Response:
Devin
1. Fetched a workable repository online for the existing approach using Alpha Go.
2. Read through the directory and identified potential components to replace for evaluation, focusing on the Agent, Neural Network, and Self-play procedure.
    a. Excluded replacing the Self-play procedure due to workload and its undeniable effectiveness.
3. Experimentation and implementation of different neural network structures: 
    a. CNN, 
    b. Convolutional ResNet, 
    c. Fully Connected neural networks
4. Neural network tuning and analysis


June Zheng
1. Searched the benchmarks we can use for the final model and proposed to use a random agent and a heuristic agent to evaluate performance
2. Designed the agent we used to replace the existing Monte Carlo Tree Search in the AlphaZero algorithm
    a. Found a paper on “Fictitious Self-Play in Extensive-Form Games” and decided to adapt its maximum likelihood approach in keeping track of the actions chosen during simulations
    b. The final agent (called FPA agent) has the following components:
         I. Forward Search Tree
         II. Limited Depth Lookahead
         III. Sparse Sampling with probability output from policy network
    c. Integrated the agent with the policy network and value network framework
         I. Action counts turned into action probabilities to train the policy network
         II. State values for leaf nodes at maximum depth are approximated with the value network
3. Programmed the new FPA decision-making search algorithm and combined it into the existing game and self-play framework of the repository that we found
4. Trained the FPA agent with the original neural network for both 6x6 and 8x8 boards
5. Tested the performance of the trained agent versus a random player and the heuristic agent

Selina
1. Read through the directory and identified potential components to replace for evaluation, focusing on the Agent, Neural Network, and Self-play procedure.
    a. Excluded replacing the Self-play procedure due to workload and its undeniable effectiveness.
2. Designed benchmarks to evaluate the models systematically:
    a. Developed random and heuristic benchmark (10+ rules) models.  
    b. Adjusted the heuristic to human-level difficulty (reduced to 3 rules) for meaningful evaluation, manually tested the heuristic, achieving a ~50% win rate, simulating a mediocre human player.
3. Evaluated updated models (ResNN and FPA) against the benchmarks
    a. Analyzed the FPA training process and noted significant differences in training design, making direct comparisons difficult
4. Created a new model combining FPA with the original Neural Network to control for training differences
5. Combined ResNeural Network with Fictitious Play Agent using reward output without additional training
6. Evaluate the combined model performance against both standalone models
7. Analyze the sources of the detrimental interaction between FPA and bottleneck ResNet

Name: Nathan Lee Kuo
Question 1 Response: Implementing Q-learning to Optimize Preflop Poker
Question 6 Response: Our group worked very cohesively throughout the project. For specifics, on the code Ishan was responsible for setting up the framework of the project such as the poker simulation we used to test, a basic q-learning algorithm, along with a lot of the data processing code. Akshath mainly focused on optimizing the q-learning algorithm by adding in features such as the code factoring in table position, different raise sizes, the current pot size, tilt factor, opponent table position and stack size, opponent belief states, ensuring the q table was properly storing and factoring in information, and opponent aggressiveness. Nathan focused on tuning the reward function specifically ensuring that the reward function would reward plays from aggressive positions with good hands and penalize bad plays from poor positions with bad hands along with ensuring the reward function benefited good plays in terms of poker game theory by adjusting reward sizes and training sizes. Nathan was also responsible for a lot of the debugging in the program as originally the results were not what we expected. The entire group was responsible for the editing and presenting of the slides.

Name: Cameron Allen Camp
Question 1 Response: Ear Training Suite for Music Students
Question 6 Response:
Ian Lasic-Ellis: 
For our project, I focused on implementing the POMDP class for the assessment tool. For the POMDP, I worked on defining the state, action, and observation space to fit our problem. Additionally, I adjusted the skill-level ranges and cutoff values in our transitions simulation to generate more realistic transition probabilities. A few other key components I implemented for the POMDP are the discrete state filter for belief updating and the expected information gain to guide decision making for actions.

I also focused on building the Q-learning algorithm for generating a bach-style melody. A key element of this implementation was figuring out how to structure the Q-table as a nested dictionary which mapped our states to actions. This created a much easier approach to update and index into the Q-table. Additionally, I compiled the quantitative data for the analysis of the POMDP and Q-learning tools. Collectively, we worked together to structure and debug each component of our project. 


Cameron Camp:
My contributions towards the project consisted primarily of implementing the LSTM to make note predictions. I also modeled and implemented the code that processes MIDI files as sequences of musical events, where each event encodes a note pitch and duration as a combined one-hot vector, and transcribed the Bach Piano piece we used as data. I also worked extensively on the modeling of each tool we created, particularly regarding the problem definition for the MDP+LSTM for generating melodies, including the definition and discretization of the state and action space to a limited range of possible midi pitches and durations. This prevented the q-table from exploding due to having too many states and actions, and allowed for efficient q-learning. As a TA for MUSIC 24A (Ear Training), I also worked to formulate the problem statements for the POMDP and Multi-Armed Bandit Interval Recognition tools, defining the action-space through determining valid interval questions and converting pitches to output frequencies. Collectively, we each assisted in the coding and debugging of each tool.

Dominic Borg:
For this project, I focused on implementing the multi-armed bandit portion of the tool,  which formed the basis for the “Practice” mode in the GUI. I also constructed and debugged the simulator that we used to generate transition and observation probabilities for the POMDP. This involved determining cutoffs for each skill level, simulating agents that fit within each range, and recording how often these agents answered questions correctly and how often they transitioned to a different skill level after a given number of simulated questions. Finally, I implemented and debugged the code for creating the GUI (shown in the video), which allows users to interact with the practice and assessment tools in a fluid and appealing manner. This involved creating multiple threads to run the user interface and update beliefs simultaneously. I also helped with general debugging of each portion of the project.

Question 6 Response:
Danny worked on the Q-Learning program, experimenting with different techniques to improve performance. He had a lot of fun integrating heuristics into Q-learning, experimenting with different rewards, and trying out Deep-Q learning which integrated deep learning with q learning, even though it ultimately failed. 
Joshua implemented the baseline random policy, look ahead with rollouts, and created evaluation charts for the approaches. He brought together all of our data from each policy, and graphically displayed them so that we could have a better understanding of how to move forward. 
Hlumelo worked on vanilla MCTS. He then worked with Josh and Danny to incorporate the learnings from their Q-learning and Lookahead policies with the MCTS — namely redesigning the reward function and integrating the custom inference into the rollout/simulation phase of the MCTS.

Name: George Robert Dimopoulos
Question 1 Response: Employing Monte Carlo Tree Search for Aircraft Collision Avoidance during Temporary Flight Restrictions
Question 6 Response: Esau's contributions consist of the initial idea for the formulation of aircraft collision avoidance as an MDP and the use of Monte Carlo Tree Search. He also defined the structure of the problem using POMDPS.jl, as well as defining the transition and reward models of the problem based on the positions, velocities, and trajectories of both aircraft; specifically the formulation of rewards calculating distance and direction and propagating the change in position. RLES-SISLES.jl did not exactly fit within creating the simulation for our project, so further work was done creating simulations of aircraft collisions for our project to use as initial states for our MDP. George's contributions consist of drafting the abstract, writing the introduction, conducting the literature review, analyzing the simulation outputs, and forming the conclusion. Furthermore, he applied the idea of aircraft collision avoidance to TFRs, and defined the action space that best fits TFR scenarios. Analysis was conducted on different exploration constants and iterations for the Monte Carlo Tree Search, along with their effectiveness. In all, we agree that we conducted roughly equal amounts of work on this project.

Thomas and Jon created assets for the video while Jack edited it. We were a good team!

CS 238 | Final Project Summary of Group Member Contributions | 12.04.2024
Connect Four Conundrum
Jasmine Gonzalez & Kadija Ismail

Name: Hunter F Hollenbeck
Question 1 Response: Euchre Bot
Question 6 Response:
Hayden: Euchre is a trick-based card game played with four players, and it consists of a bidding phase and a playing phase. Hayden coded the underlying game mechanics for the game. He implemented the various classes to store Player, Bidding, and Playing information and created the functions for the interactions among the players and the game. An updated game state was maintained to store data from the game. Like Hunter and Avery, Hayden contributed to the final video. 

Hunter: Hunter contributed to the project by optimizing the code and filtering the action space down to a mere eight actions. The efforts done by Hunter sped up our code by a factor of five and allowed us to test and validate code much faster with vectorized numpy operations. Hunter’s ideas with the action space were crucial to creating a policy in a reasonable amount of time while still creating a coherent agent for playing 

Avery: Avery contributed to the project by implementing the code to run many simulations of playing a round of Euchre. This was helpful both to get data at the beginning of our algorithm when all of the players were playing randomly, and then at the end of the algorithm to test our policy against our baseline. Avery also implemented the Q-learning algorithm and the logic to extract the state, reward, action, and next state from the play function in the player class. Like Hunter and Hayden, Avery also helped with the final video.

Contributions: 
Justin: Predominantly worked on the special card functionality in Unity, implementing skips, reverses, draw-2s, draw-4s, and draw-2 and draw-4 stacking logic from scratch. Name: Lea Wang-Tomic
Question 1 Response: WORDLE Solver
Question 6 Response:
Lea implemented logic to set up our vocabulary by loading in the words we will use as our possible guesses. She downloaded words from a dataset containing an extensive list of English words (the words corpus) through the Natural Language Processing Toolkit (NLTK) Python library. After retrieving those words, she filtered to keep only the five-letter words. Additionally, she ranked the words based on frequency in decreasing order from one of the largest collections of words in English text (the brown corpus). To clarify, the word that appears the most frequently in the brown corpus will be our highest-ranked word. Additionally she set up the coding environment and implemented the plots. 

Sade implemented logic for the preliminary model. For example she hard coded how to filter out words that don't match the feedback given by that guess. For example, if a letter is green, keep words with that letter in the same position. If a letter is yellow, keep words containing that letter but in a different position. If a letter is gray, remove words containing that letter. Additionally she hard coded the feedback making the result array indices be 'grey', 'yellow', or 'green' depending on the choice.

Kenny implemented logic for the reinforcement learning agent.  He created the class WordleEnv that simulates the game Wordle. The environment initialized with a target word and a list of valid words. It tracks the number of attempts, letters known to be in correct and incorrect positions, and incorrect letters. It includes methods to reset the game state, provide feedback based on guesses, and progress the game state. He also implemented the Q learning class and trained the agent based on Q learning. 

We all did a few slides each for the presentation in our video.

Name: Tanav Ohal
Question 1 Response: Unbeatable Checkers Bot 9000
Question 6 Response:
Arun implemented the MDP class to model the checkers game as a Markov Decision process which included the implementation of states, rewards, actions, and transition functions. This provided the basic structure to implement the MCTS structure to allow simulations and policy evaluations. This set of functions allowed for the bot to handle complex board dynamics and states. Arun also worked on the reward function which assigned a numerical value to a possible board state based on factors like captures, king promotions, board control, and piece counts. The reward function is at the core of the MCTS algorithm as the future moves are determined by selecting the most optimal strategy with the highest reward. The implementation also takes into account different states of the game, whether that is a layout closer to the initial layout or closer to the endgame state. The reward function is dynamically updated based on these states and the available pieces still in the game.

Tanav developed the MCTBot class which is the base of the MCTS algorithm. The class stores the state of the game at each node, tracks the number of visits, and keeps track of the cumulative rewards values for the next decision to be made. Tanav implemented methods to manage child nodes from the tree, calculate the UCB values, and balance exploration and exploitation in different states of the game. Tanav also implemented the get_valid_moves function which calculates the possible moves for a given piece. This included regular moves, captures, and multi-jumps with a recursive search. He designed the function to account for normal pieces and kings and their movement rules, incorporating board boundaries, and visited positions to avoid loops in multi-jump scenarios.

Nishant worked on the initialize_board function which sets up the initialization of the checkers board for the user to view, this includes the correct placement of user and bot pieces on their respective sides of the board in the correct alternating layout. The correct initial layout is critical to the future board states and gameplay logic. He also worked on the play_checkers function which serves as the primary gameplay loop managing turns, move execution, and game-ending conditions. This function is important to handle user input, bot decision making, and board updates. The last function he implemented was the move_pieces function which handles the movement of pieces on the board, including regular moves, captures, and multi-jumps which update the board state as needed. Lastly, Nishant worked on creating the slides for the presentation with the rest of the group and ensuring that the slides were concise and clear.
